case_name: Susan D. Royce v. State Farm Fire and Casualty Company
transcript_text:
This is case 2250330, Susan D. Royce v. State Farm, Fire, and Casualty Company.

Good morning, Attorney Boosier. I understand you reserve one minute.

That's a great draw.

And for the record, Justice Will is disqualified on this case.

Please proceed.

Ladies and gentlemen, members of the Court, may it please the Court.

My name is Emil Boosier, Jr. I represent Susan D. Royce, the appellant in this case.

In New Hampshire, the existence of uninsured motorist coverage or UIM coverage

and umbrella insurance policies determined not only by the language of the insurance policy,

but also by the statutory mandate of inclusion of UIM coverage

as found in our Financial Responsibility Act.

Could you lift the microphone?

I'm sorry.

Speak up a little.

Is that better?

Yes.

I can speak up, too.

This Court has recognized that the Financial Responsibility Act

has been liberally construed to accomplish its legislative purpose

of protecting persons in automobile accidents from losses

which because of the tort users' lack of liability insurance go uncompensated.

In this case, the specific provision of the Financial Responsibility Act

that we're looking at is the version of RSA 264151.

That is applicable to this case when my client's sister Catherine Royce

purchased her umbrella policy in 2000 and amended it thereafter in 2001.

At that time, RSA 264151 provided that when an insured elects to purchase liability insurance

in an amount greater than the minimum coverage required by RSA 25961,

his uninsured motorist coverage shall automatically be equal to the liability coverage elected.

For purposes of this paragraph, umbrella or excess policies that provide excess limits

to policies described in 25961 shall also provide uninsured motorist coverage

equal to the limits of liability purchased unless the named insured rejects such coverage.

On a plain reading of this statute, the triggering event for the inclusion of UIM coverage

is when an insured elects to purchase liability insurance

in an amount greater than the minimum required coverage.

The trial court in this case correctly found that the event that requires the inclusion of UIM coverage

in an umbrella policy is when an insured elects to purchase liability insurance.

But the trial court erred when it equated that phrase,

elects to purchase as only meaning the initial decision to purchase liability insurance.

So what do we make of the language in the initial decision, the initial contract

that provided an acknowledgement that the rejection applied to all future renewals

and all replacement policies? What do we do with that language?

Well, that language contravenes the dictates of the statute.

The statute did not permit a prospective rejection of UIM coverage

to operate every time in the future for every time that an insured elects to purchase liability insurance.

The election to purchase liability insurance is a decision.

As the trial court ruled, the word elects is to choose or to decide.

So when someone makes a decision and chooses to purchase liability insurance

in excess of the minimum requirements, that happens, of course, when you initiate a policy

because you pull out your checkbook, you pay a premium.

But then when the renewal comes up and it's time to make a decision

about whether you're going to purchase additional liability coverage

for that successive policy period, every insured has to make a decision.

Am I going to purchase this liability insurance for this additional policy period?

And that's a decision that is made, it's an election that is made to purchase insurance

when you track the language of RSA 26415 as it exists at the time.

That is the trigger that mandates the inclusion of UIM coverage.

Do we know, do we know if, I recognize that the policy changed

because of different vehicles or different drivers, but setting that aside,

did State Farm change the policy or coverage over that 20-year time frame?

Well, the policy, you know, setting aside the addition of a vehicle

or the detraction of the vehicle, the addition of a youthful driver

was a very significant amendment and policy change that took place in November of 2001.

The policy had been in effect for a year and a month.

And then a decision was made by Catherine Royce to increase her umbrella coverage

from $1 million to $5 million.

When she did that, she made an election to purchase additional liability coverage

in excess of the statutory minimum required amount.

That triggered that sentence of RSA 264151,

mandating the inclusion of UIM coverage.

And if you look to the second sentence or the last sentence of 264151,

it says, you know, for purposes of umbrella policies that provide

excess limits to policies described in RSA 25961,

they shall also provide uninsured motor's coverage

equal to the limits of liability purchased.

Equal to the limits of liability purchased would mean

that $5 million of umbrella coverage,

she had made the election to purchase liability coverage

in excess of the statutory minimums,

and therefore her policy automatically included UIM coverage at that time.

Does the election to purchase language even apply to that last sentence?

I'm sorry?

Does the election to purchase language even apply?

Take my other advice here.

Move my microphone.

Does the election to purchase language even apply

to that last sentence of 264151?

Well, I understand the State Department argues it didn't.

The trial court found that it did.

And the reason that the trial court properly found that it did

is because the final sentence of 264151

was added in response to Wilkinson,

the court's decision on Wilkinson.

And when they added that sentence, they said,

this is of this paragraph.

So they weren't saying that this is going to be a stand-alone sentence

that doesn't jive with the remainder of the paragraph.

They said, for purposes of this paragraph.

And there is no triggering event concluded

within that final sentence.

So like the trial court found, the triggering event

has to be the election to purchase coverage.

But again, the trial court got it wrong when it said

that the election to purchase coverage only happens once

when an insured purchases a policy of insurance

over and above the minimum mandatory coverage of 2550.

If I could just give you an example, I'm sorry.

What other instances of election to purchase coverage

happened in this case beyond increasing the limits in 2001?

Every year when a renewal was made of the policy,

the insured has to make a decision

to elect to purchase additional insurance.

So every renewal period, the statute applies again.

Is that what you're saying?

Every renewal, for every time someone makes the decision

to purchase additional insurance for additional policy period,

tracking the language, that's exactly what it says.

So why would we be looking at the version of the statute

that existed at the time of each one of those events?

I'm not understanding why the parties agreed

to this version of the statute applying in this case.

Well, it's because the pertinent conduct

that would have caused the inclusion of the UIM coverage

had not occurred at the time that this version of the statute

was in place.

It wasn't until 2007 when the statute was amended

that it changed and altered the terms of insurance,

of how insurance policies are written in New Hampshire.

But the 2007 or 2008 amendment, whichever you want to call it,

would not have any retrospective effect

upon the conduct of the parties at the time

that the contract was entered into back in 2001

when the insurance contract was altered in 2001.

So once this court finds that the mandatory inclusion

of coverage applied, for instance, with respect

to the $5 million umbrella coverage purchase in 2001,

every renewal of the policy is going to include

the umbrella coverage that was statutory mandated

to be included in the policy back in 2001

when she increased her limits to that amount.

And then every success of renewal is just renewing

what existed prior, which was $5 million

worth of uninsured motorist coverage.

Sort of sounds like you wanted both ways.

You're saying that each renewal is a different policy,

and yet if that is the case, then the last policy

that your client renewed would have been the year

that the event occurred and the current version

of the statute would apply.

No, I'm not asking to have it both ways

and just asking the law as it was written back in 2000 and 2001

when Catherine Royce purchased the initial umbrella policy

and increased the limits in a way that mandated

the inclusion of UIM coverage to be enforced.

Thereafter, in 2007 or 2008, when the legislation was passed

saying that from here on in, rejections of insurance coverage

are going to apply to amendments and renewals,

which it did not say before.

Once that statute was amended, it was well within

State Farm's ability to contact its insurance agents

and say get another rejection from anyone

that has umbrella coverage to make sure

that they are rejecting umbrella coverage still.

They could have done that. They didn't do it.

And there was nothing that Catherine Royce did

after she increased her policy in this to ever express

her rejection of UIM coverage with respect to $5 million

worth of liability coverage that she purchased,

which was mandated by the statute to include.

But she never made a written request to add that

coverage pursuant to the original policy

that she signed, correct?

Pursuant to State Farm's policy, yes.

Pursuant to the financial responsibility, no.

She wasn't required, well, according to financial

responsibility, actually was required to reject

such coverage when she increased her limits

to $5 million. And she never rejected such coverage.

She never rejected the statutory mandate

that her UIM coverage when she elected

to purchase $5 million worth of coverage

would not have included the additional

$5 million worth of UIM coverage.

At any rate, I appreciate the opportunity

to flip a decision by Justice Will.

You very recently affirmed one of his decisions,

and I think it was Braxmore.

So this might be one of the last times

you get an opportunity to reverse it.

Any more questions?

Yes, and this is not idle curiosity.

What is the, roughly, what is the amount

of the UIM claim in this case?

My client was terribly injured in the accident.

She broke her hip.

Well, we're not a jury.

I'm just looking for the number.

You know, with the amount of time that has passed

in this case, knowing now that, you know,

my client still needs, that didn't need

the assistance of a cane to get around when she goes

to a restaurant or to the mall now,

she gets pushed around in a wheelchair.

I think it's very high, but I'm not at liberty

to say what the number is without conferring

with her again at this point.

So I'm sorry.

I'm not trying to talk the question.

I understand your answer.

That might have been an even more inappropriate

question than mine.

Thank you.

Thank you very much.

Attorney Boos here.

Attorney Conner.

May it please the court?

I'm Doreen Conner and I represent State Farm

in this appeal.

I will take questions at any time.

The plaintiff's appeal asks this court

to reform her sister's umbrella policy

to add UIM coverage which her sister rejected

in writing and never purchased.

Insurance companies are in the business of selling insurance.

Head Ms. Royce wanted to purchase umbrella UIM coverage

at any time during the 20 years she had this policy.

She had every opportunity to do so.

And as this court noted in Swain at 150 New Hampshire,

the bedrock principle of the UM statute

is to allow policy holders the choice to purchase coverage.

She did not exercise that choice.

On appeal, the plaintiff makes two arguments.

First, she asserts that the statute

required a new UIM umbrella waiver with each renewal,

each auto replacement, each driver change,

each policy limit change.

Because the 1991 version of the statute

contained no language addressing the

duration timing of an insured waiver,

the fact that there's no requirement in the statute

saying you have to renew and get a new waiver

on this occasion or after this many years

doesn't impose one.

It indicates that there is no requirement whatsoever.

This court noted in state versus Surrell,

cited by the trial court,

that silence in a statute doesn't create an obligation.

In this case, the statute did not require

and did not describe what the waiver might be.

The legislature just said pursuant to Wilkinson,

now umbrella carers must offer the option,

but it is up to the insured

and if the insured rejects it, that's it.

Now, with regard to this argument,

with regard to the second sentence,

elects to purchase,

I agree with Justice Gould that that does not

apply at all in this case.

And in fact, that's what this court said

in Santos versus Metropolitan.

This case is entirely governed by that case.

In Santos, this court went through the three sentences

in RSA 264.50.

You talked about the fact that the first sentence

applies to minimum level policies, 25.50 policies.

And it requires equivalent amounts,

dollar amounts, and in equivalent limits,

scope of coverage.

The second sentence talks about those policies,

which are primary auto policies,

but more than 25.

And back at this time,

and also at the time of Santos case,

if you had one of those primary auto policies,

say it's 100,

then you had to have the carrier had to give you

the same dollar amounts,

but not the same scope.

That gives rise to the Gisoni case,

where we have a difference

between liability coverage,

not being provided 50 miles into Mexico,

but no UIM.

This court noted that the carrier was allowed to do that

because the financial responsibility statute

differentiated between dollars and scope.

Santos then says that the third sentence,

the final sentence,

is the only sentence that applies to umbrella policies.

That doesn't include elect to purchase language.

And it clearly doesn't talk about having equal dollar limits.

It says that it must be offered to have equal limits,

i.e. scope.

And it doesn't talk about having to mandate

equal amounts, dollar amounts,

because the insured has the right to reject that.

The insured doesn't have that reject

with a primary auto policy.

It is forced by the legislature

to make sure it has equivalent

dollar amounts of UIM

and liability coverage.

Umbrella policies are a whole new different animal.

This court recognized that in Wilkinson,

the legislature has continued to recognize it.

And therefore, this case is controlled entirely by Santos

and the trial court should be affirmed.

Should apply any part in our analysis

that the language used by State Farm

frames the issue as

an option to purchase this additional coverage,

whereas the statute states that

the coverage is included

unless it's rejected.

I think it's the same thing, Your Honor.

The application form indicates

that in keeping with the laws of the state

I have been offered the opportunity to purchase

and that I reject it.

I think that's in keeping exactly

with what the statute requires

in terms of

offering it unless the name insured

rejects it.

Well, I'll say it as my concern.

My concern is that it frames the issue

in a way that makes it less likely

that the insured will

maintain the right given by statute

because it's framed in terms of

do you want to spend this additional money?

I agree that substantively

you arrive at the same place.

I see I'm mystifying you.

If this court were to find that

the case is not governed by Santos,

which I don't think it should do,

the trial court have found

on alternative grounds

that even if this elects

to purchase language applies

that the election to purchase occurs

with the first time you purchase it.

And he noted that in this case

every year Ms. Roy's

got a renewal certificate.

She didn't get a new policy.

She got a renewal certificate.

And the policy

term, the policy period

stayed the same for 20 years.

The policy number stayed the same

for 20 years.

And so she elected to purchase

this umbrella policy

only once and maintained it

throughout the years.

So that's your explanation for why the

1991 version of the statute

applies?

Are we bound by that?

I think

statutory construction

is the final arbiter of this

and if this court wants to apply

the more recent statute

that is signed with my client

in the argument below I believe

Attorney Boussier conceded

he has a much harder row

under the new statute

which makes it very clear

that when an insured

signs off on a waiver

that waiver continues into the future.

I want to talk a little bit

about the argument that somehow

in 2007

means that what State Farm did

in this case in 2000

was not permissible

and that's not true.

This court noted in the Cicier

Loone case which dealt with

municipal immunity

for the sport of skiing

you construed the statute

and you said it included snow

borders. Later on in time

the legislature agreed with you

and added that to the statute

and I believe one of my colleagues

who is a plaintiff's lawyer

wanted to make an argument

that change in the legislature

meant that originally

snow borders were not included

and this court said no

with simply a clarification.

The same applies here.

The legislature clarified

that a written waiver

would apply to infinitum

and that's also because

the legislative changed there

was an additional onus

on the insurance companies

where it's under the 1991

you could reject UIM

but you could do it orally.

What's your response to the notion

that the increase to 5 million

dollars in coverage

would have triggered the opportunity

to elect

whether that extended or not

or waive it?

Yes Your Honor, that was decided

by this court in O'Donnell

versus Allstate was under a different statute

but this court rejected

the argument that attorney

Booster here is making here

and a treaty more is set made in that case

which is when there's an increase in dollars

he argued I believe it was a

substantiality or materiality

increased change

that should trigger a new

umbrella waiver form

and this court rejected that

and said no it does not.

I thought it was a reduction in coverage

in O'Donnell Am I?

Yes but still it was a material change

and so he argued that that should have triggered

a new waiver

because getting a new waiver was the only way

he was going to get

an opportunity to purchase

umbrella coverage after the fact.

So setting aside the other

changes with drivers

youthful drivers vehicles

did State Farm ever

change anything about the policy

over that period of time?

That's not in the record Your Honor

I don't know that.

I do know that what is in the record

are renewal certificates for each policy

year and there's one

certified policy but I did not

the court did not

track any changes.

You agree that we're supposed to

construed the statute

liberally to effectuate its purpose?

I think you construed the statute

as a matter of statutory construction.

The liberal

construction with regard to financial

responsibility

coverage in terms of

innocent victims

doesn't apply in the UIM

context and that's what this court said in

Swain.

You know it's totally up

to in this case

either Susan Royce herself

to purchase coverage to protect herself

or her sister Catherine Royce

to protect the occupants of her vehicle.

They chose not to

and it's not this court's

job to reform the policy

to override that

conscious choice and so no

I don't think that there needs to be any

liberal construction of the statute

rather just a

traditional interpretation

of the statute and I think it's

controlled by Santos and O'Donnell.

Why doesn't the

the purchase

of four million dollars in additional

coverage trigger

the requirement

that

an equivalent amount be offered

as UIM

coverage?

Because it was an amendment to the policy.

It was not a new policy.

But it purchases additional

coverage?

It is a purchase of additional liability

coverage but it was not a purchase

of additional UIM.

The additional

purchase of coverage is no

different than adding another

driver adding additional

vehicles all of those

increase the underwriting risk

and they do not trigger

an additional umbrella

waiver because the statute didn't

require it because Ms. Royce

when she executed this

application understood that

State Farm was relying on the fact

that her waiver would apply

to this policy

renewals and replacement

permits and she continued to

maintain the same policy

even after she amended it on a renewal

basis for 20 years.

She never revoked her waiver.

That's all she had to do

and she chose not to do it.

The one other thing that

I want to talk briefly about here

deals with the argument

that renewal and amendment

are somehow different

and that

the policy application

which states that it would apply

to future renewals and all replacement

policies didn't cover an amendment.

Again, this court addressed that

in O'Donnell and it

said that it would be

arbitrary to indicate

that a policy that is renewed

with amended

terms was somehow mutually exclusive.

You can have the very same

policy with amended

terms.

In this case, State Farm's waiver applied

to future renewals and replacements.

O'Donnell supplied to future renewals

for continuations of my policy

and this court noted

in O'Donnell that that

applied in both settings.

It did not trigger a new

waiver requirement.

I would also note

that we referenced in our brief

the Bufard versus State Farm file

the critical issue in this case

was not raised in that case

but presumably

if the application process

was inconsistent with state law

somebody would have raised it.

It was the same

State Farm umbrella waiver.

We had a renewal.

We had an accident

and the court had no problem

enforcing it

and those pleadings

were all before this board

as part of the appendix.

The major issue in Bufard

had to deal with agency law.

Again, in summary

this case...

May I just ask one more issue

of statutory construction?

One more issue.

The use of the present

tends for rejects.

Does that

I mean

the plaintiff points to

in the second sentence

a lax to purchase and then

in the third sentence

it's rejects as opposed to

said?

I don't believe it makes a difference

because we also have

equal to the limits

of liability purchased past tense.

I don't think

it changes the outcome.

I think you only need

one UIM waiver

and that's what we have in this case.

So again

in summary

the choice to purchase umbrella coverage

was in the hands not only

short in this case but also the

plaintiff, neither of them chose

that option.

The Hampshire legislature has

recognized that option.

We believe that this court should affirm

the trial court's decision.

Thank you attorney Conner.

Your Honor.

Everybody else?

I just want to touch on the O'Donnell case.

The O'Donnell case is not controlling here.

The O'Donnell case was decided

after the 2007 amendment

which then for the first time ever

said that a rejection

is going to have prospective effect

with respect to renewals and

amendments to the policy.

If the legislature did not think that the words

renewal and amendment were different things

they would have simply just said renewals

but they chose the two different things.

Renewals and amendments.

And that's important in this case because

at the time that Catherine Royce increased

her policy from $1 million to $5 million

which was a substantive

to the insurance policy

the law did not provide

that waivers would apply to future

amendments to the policy.

Also

the waiver that State Farm created

did not expressly apply

to future amendments to the policy.

State Farm could have written its

prospective rejection

in a way that would have applied to

amendments but it did not.

And therefore when a reasonable insured

makes a decision to increase their coverage

to $5 million they could look at that

waiver that State Farm produced

and conclude that that waiver

But Donald did not address that issue

in any way. Thank you very much

Turner Bruce here. Case is submitted.

segments.text: This is case 2250330, Susan D. Royce v. State Farm, Fire, and Casualty Company.
Good morning, Attorney Boosier. I understand you reserve one minute.
That's a great draw.
And for the record, Justice Will is disqualified on this case.
Please proceed.
Ladies and gentlemen, members of the Court, may it please the Court.
My name is Emil Boosier, Jr. I represent Susan D. Royce, the appellant in this case.
In New Hampshire, the existence of uninsured motorist coverage or UIM coverage
and umbrella insurance policies determined not only by the language of the insurance policy,
but also by the statutory mandate of inclusion of UIM coverage
as found in our Financial Responsibility Act.
Could you lift the microphone?
I'm sorry.
Speak up a little.
Is that better?
Yes.
I can speak up, too.
This Court has recognized that the Financial Responsibility Act
has been liberally construed to accomplish its legislative purpose
of protecting persons in automobile accidents from losses
which because of the tort users' lack of liability insurance go uncompensated.
In this case, the specific provision of the Financial Responsibility Act
that we're looking at is the version of RSA 264151.
That is applicable to this case when my client's sister Catherine Royce
purchased her umbrella policy in 2000 and amended it thereafter in 2001.
At that time, RSA 264151 provided that when an insured elects to purchase liability insurance
in an amount greater than the minimum coverage required by RSA 25961,
his uninsured motorist coverage shall automatically be equal to the liability coverage elected.
For purposes of this paragraph, umbrella or excess policies that provide excess limits
to policies described in 25961 shall also provide uninsured motorist coverage
equal to the limits of liability purchased unless the named insured rejects such coverage.
On a plain reading of this statute, the triggering event for the inclusion of UIM coverage
is when an insured elects to purchase liability insurance
in an amount greater than the minimum required coverage.
The trial court in this case correctly found that the event that requires the inclusion of UIM coverage
in an umbrella policy is when an insured elects to purchase liability insurance.
But the trial court erred when it equated that phrase,
elects to purchase as only meaning the initial decision to purchase liability insurance.
So what do we make of the language in the initial decision, the initial contract
that provided an acknowledgement that the rejection applied to all future renewals
and all replacement policies? What do we do with that language?
Well, that language contravenes the dictates of the statute.
The statute did not permit a prospective rejection of UIM coverage
to operate every time in the future for every time that an insured elects to purchase liability insurance.
The election to purchase liability insurance is a decision.
As the trial court ruled, the word elects is to choose or to decide.
So when someone makes a decision and chooses to purchase liability insurance
in excess of the minimum requirements, that happens, of course, when you initiate a policy
because you pull out your checkbook, you pay a premium.
But then when the renewal comes up and it's time to make a decision
about whether you're going to purchase additional liability coverage
for that successive policy period, every insured has to make a decision.
Am I going to purchase this liability insurance for this additional policy period?
And that's a decision that is made, it's an election that is made to purchase insurance
when you track the language of RSA 26415 as it exists at the time.
That is the trigger that mandates the inclusion of UIM coverage.
Do we know, do we know if, I recognize that the policy changed
because of different vehicles or different drivers, but setting that aside,
did State Farm change the policy or coverage over that 20-year time frame?
Well, the policy, you know, setting aside the addition of a vehicle
or the detraction of the vehicle, the addition of a youthful driver
was a very significant amendment and policy change that took place in November of 2001.
The policy had been in effect for a year and a month.
And then a decision was made by Catherine Royce to increase her umbrella coverage
from $1 million to $5 million.
When she did that, she made an election to purchase additional liability coverage
in excess of the statutory minimum required amount.
That triggered that sentence of RSA 264151,
mandating the inclusion of UIM coverage.
And if you look to the second sentence or the last sentence of 264151,
it says, you know, for purposes of umbrella policies that provide
excess limits to policies described in RSA 25961,
they shall also provide uninsured motor's coverage
equal to the limits of liability purchased.
Equal to the limits of liability purchased would mean
that $5 million of umbrella coverage,
she had made the election to purchase liability coverage
in excess of the statutory minimums,
and therefore her policy automatically included UIM coverage at that time.
Does the election to purchase language even apply to that last sentence?
I'm sorry?
Does the election to purchase language even apply?
Take my other advice here.
Move my microphone.
Does the election to purchase language even apply
to that last sentence of 264151?
Well, I understand the State Department argues it didn't.
The trial court found that it did.
And the reason that the trial court properly found that it did
is because the final sentence of 264151
was added in response to Wilkinson,
the court's decision on Wilkinson.
And when they added that sentence, they said,
this is of this paragraph.
So they weren't saying that this is going to be a stand-alone sentence
that doesn't jive with the remainder of the paragraph.
They said, for purposes of this paragraph.
And there is no triggering event concluded
within that final sentence.
So like the trial court found, the triggering event
has to be the election to purchase coverage.
But again, the trial court got it wrong when it said
that the election to purchase coverage only happens once
when an insured purchases a policy of insurance
over and above the minimum mandatory coverage of 2550.
If I could just give you an example, I'm sorry.
What other instances of election to purchase coverage
happened in this case beyond increasing the limits in 2001?
Every year when a renewal was made of the policy,
the insured has to make a decision
to elect to purchase additional insurance.
So every renewal period, the statute applies again.
Is that what you're saying?
Every renewal, for every time someone makes the decision
to purchase additional insurance for additional policy period,
tracking the language, that's exactly what it says.
So why would we be looking at the version of the statute
that existed at the time of each one of those events?
I'm not understanding why the parties agreed
to this version of the statute applying in this case.
Well, it's because the pertinent conduct
that would have caused the inclusion of the UIM coverage
had not occurred at the time that this version of the statute
was in place.
It wasn't until 2007 when the statute was amended
that it changed and altered the terms of insurance,
of how insurance policies are written in New Hampshire.
But the 2007 or 2008 amendment, whichever you want to call it,
would not have any retrospective effect
upon the conduct of the parties at the time
that the contract was entered into back in 2001
when the insurance contract was altered in 2001.
So once this court finds that the mandatory inclusion
of coverage applied, for instance, with respect
to the $5 million umbrella coverage purchase in 2001,
every renewal of the policy is going to include
the umbrella coverage that was statutory mandated
to be included in the policy back in 2001
when she increased her limits to that amount.
And then every success of renewal is just renewing
what existed prior, which was $5 million
worth of uninsured motorist coverage.
Sort of sounds like you wanted both ways.
You're saying that each renewal is a different policy,
and yet if that is the case, then the last policy
that your client renewed would have been the year
that the event occurred and the current version
of the statute would apply.
No, I'm not asking to have it both ways
and just asking the law as it was written back in 2000 and 2001
when Catherine Royce purchased the initial umbrella policy
and increased the limits in a way that mandated
